Spatial Regression Assessment 3156567m · Fabian Moreno · Urban Analytics Msc

What drives Edinburgh Airbnb prices? A spatial regression analysis

Advanced Tools for Urban Analytics · Prof. Qunshan Zhao · March 2026

Overview This report fits OLS, Spatial Lag (SAR), Spatial Error (SEM), GWR, and mGWR models to 4,602 Edinburgh Airbnb listings to identify the main drivers of nightly price. The key finding is that accommodation capacity is the strongest predictor, and its effect varies considerably across Edinburgh — which is why mGWR ends up as the best-performing model.
4,602
listings after cleaning
12
predictor variables
0.617
best adj. R² (mGWR)
0.105
Moran's I (OLS resid.)

1. Data and variable selection

The dataset comes from Inside Airbnb (September 2025) and originally had 4,936 listings and 79 columns. After dropping rows with missing values in key variables, 4,602 listings remained. Price was log-transformed because the raw distribution is heavily right-skewed (max = £18,465, mean = £300). The log version is much closer to normal and gives the OLS model better properties (Figure 1).

Figure 1. Raw vs log-transformed price. The original distribution is almost entirely compressed near zero, while the log version is roughly bell-shaped and suitable for regression.

Variable selection was theory-driven — I only kept variables with a plausible link to price. Three amenity dummies were created by parsing the free-text amenities column: indoor fireplace, patio/balcony, and dedicated workspace. Only indoor fireplace turned out to be significant and stayed in the model. Variables present in over 75% of listings (kitchen, washer, oven) were dropped since they offer almost no discriminating power.

Neighbourhood effects were encoded as dummies using neighbourhood_cleansed. 69 of the 111 neighbourhoods had fewer than 30 listings and were grouped into an "Other" reference category, leaving 42 neighbourhood dummies. VIF checks confirmed no serious multicollinearity (all VIFs below 10) once review_scores_rating and bedrooms were removed from earlier iterations.

2. OLS results

The OLS model explains about 48% of price variation (R² = 0.481, Adj. R² = 0.475). The strongest predictor is accommodates (β = 0.134, p < 0.001), each extra guest capacity adds roughly 13% to price. Bathrooms (β = 0.101), and instant bookability (β = 0.109) are also strongly positive. However, Room type is where you see the biggest effects: private rooms are around 37% cheaper than entire homes, shared rooms 62% cheaper.

Superhost status adds about 8% to price, which makes sense as quality signal. The negative coefficient on reviews_per_month (β = −0.037) was counterintuitive at first, but it reflects the fact that budget listings turn over guests more quickly and tend to rack up reviews faster. Indoor fireplace adds around 5% to price, which fits Edinburgh's historic tenement context.

Neighbourhood effects are large and clearly matter. New Town East and Gayfield, The Grange, and Deans Village all show premiums over 40% relative to the reference category. Muirhouse is the only neighbourhood with a significant negative coefficient (−0.195), which lines up with its socioeconomic profile (Figure 2).

Figure 2. OLS neighbourhood price premiums vs "Other" reference. Blue = statistically significant at p < 0.05. The central and affluent southern neighbourhoods consistently command the highest premiums.

3. Spatial autocorrelation

Before running spatial models, I needed to check whether the OLS residuals are spatially random. They're not. Moran's I = 0.105 (p = 0.001) using a KNN weights matrix (k = 8, row-standardised). This means listings with under- or over-predicted prices tend to cluster geographically, which violates the OLS independence assumption and justifies moving to spatial models (Figure 3).

Figure 3. Moran's I test for OLS residuals. The observed value (0.11) sits far in the right tail of the distribution, confirming significant positive spatial autocorrelation (p = 0.001).

Both LM Lag and Robust LM Lag were highly significant (p < 0.001). I ran both SAR and SEM to compare results.

4. Spatial regression (SAR and SEM)

The Spatial Lag model (SAR) added a spatially lagged dependent variable to capture neighbour price effects. The parameter ρ = 0.262 is significant, meaning hosts are influenced by nearby prices when setting their own. SAR gets to R² = 0.502, a slight improvement over OLS. The Spatial Error model (SEM) captureed spatial structure in the error term (λ = 0.325), with slightly lower R² (0.480) but better AIC (7,029 vs 7,041). Both were close. The key point here was that the substantive coefficients barely change across OLS, SAR, and SEM (Table 1).

Table 1. Coefficient comparison — OLS, SAR, SEM. *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1. Neighbourhood dummies not shown.
Variable OLS SAR SEM
Constant 4.4246*** 3.1765*** 4.4399***
accommodates 0.1336*** 0.1298*** 0.1287***
bathrooms 0.1005*** 0.1002*** 0.1059***
host_is_superhost 0.0823*** 0.0801*** 0.0800***
instant_bookable 0.1088*** 0.1088*** 0.1110***
has_indoor_fireplace 0.0531** 0.0517** 0.0510**
room_Private room −0.3716*** −0.3700*** −0.3869***
room_Shared room −0.6172*** −0.5040*** −0.5956***
reviews_per_month −0.0371*** −0.0355*** −0.0347***
number_of_reviews −0.0004*** −0.0004*** −0.0004***
availability_365 0.0002*** 0.0002*** 0.0002***
calculated_host_listings_count 0.0023*** 0.0019*** 0.0017***
ρ (Spatial Lag) — 0.2616*** —
λ (Spatial Error) — — 0.3251***

5. GWR and mGWR

The global models all assume that the relationship between each predictor and price is the same everywhere in Edinburgh. I used GWR to test that assumption by fitting local regressions at each observation using AICc-optimal bandwidth = 273 neighbours. There was substantial improvement: R² jumps to 0.630, up from 0.502 in SAR. This could mean that the relationships does varies across the city.

The mGWR test lets each variable have its own bandwidth. The most interesting result here was that accommodates gets a bandwidth of just 49 neighbours, meaning its effect is highly local. On the other hand, capacity commands a much higher premium in the city centre than in outer areas, which makes sense. At the other end, availability_365 gets a bandwidth of 4,020 and its effect is the same everywhere. mGWR ended up as the best model overall (R² = 0.663, Adj. R² = 0.617, Table 2).

Table 2. mGWR variable bandwidths. Smaller = more localised effect.
Variable Bandwidth Scale
accommodates 49 Highly localised
host_is_superhost 150 Localised
has_indoor_fireplace 160 Localised
room_Private room 476 Regional
bathrooms 637 Regional
reviews_per_month 1,069 Sub-global
calculated_host_listings_count 1,314 Sub-global
number_of_reviews 1,421 Sub-global
instant_bookable 1,563 Sub-global
availability_365 4,020 Near-global

Figure 4. Local coefficient maps for GWR (top) and mGWR (bottom). The accommodates maps show a clear centre-periphery pattern. The mGWR maps for host_is_superhost show stronger eastern effects.

6. Model comparison

Table 3. Model performance summary.
Model R² Adj. R² AIC Spatial param.
OLS 0.481 0.475 7,194 —
SEM 0.480 — 7,029 λ = 0.325***
SAR 0.502 — 7,041 ρ = 0.262***
GWR 0.630 0.585 9,483 BW = 273
mGWR ✓ 0.663 0.617 9,162 BW = 49–4,020

The global models are fairly close to each other. Moving from OLS to SAR or SEM only added a few percentage points of explained variance. There was a big jump happening when moving to GWR (+13 percentage points), which confirmed that spatial non-stationarity was the more important spatial issue in this dataset, not just spatial dependence.

By Looking at the residual maps (Figure 5), we can see how all global models show obvious clustering around the city centre and Old Town. GWR and mGWR residuals look much more randomly scattered.

Figure 5. Residual maps across all five models. Red = under-prediction, blue = over-prediction. The global models show clear spatial clustering in the city centre. GWR and mGWR are much more evenly distributed.

Limitations worth noting were: Hotel rooms and shared rooms had to be dropped from GWR and mGWR (only 21 and 19 listings respectively). A coordinate jitter was applied to manage duplicate lat/lon values where multiple listings share a building.

7. Summary

The main factors driving Edinburgh Airbnb prices are: accommodation capacity, room type, number of bathrooms, instant bookability, superhost status, and neighbourhood. Out of these, capacity is the strongest and also the most spatially variable. mGWR is the best model overall because it captures both that spatial variation and the fact that different variables operate at different geographic scales.

ATUA 2026 · Spatial Regression Assessment Inside Airbnb, Edinburgh · September 2025
